title: Impact of thermal and dissipative effects in a periodically-kicked quantum battery
authors: Sebastián V. Romero, Xi Chen, Yue Ban
abstract: Quantum batteries (QBs) have emerged as a promising route for fast energy storage and on-chip power supply in quantum devices. Given the limited analytical understanding of open Floquet QBs, we employ the kicked-Ising model as a tractable platform to systematically study its performance under realistic conditions, including finite temperature effects and environmental dissipation. Starting from Gibbs states of the transverse-field Ising model, we incorporate thermal and decoherence effects along the evolution, using both analytical and numerical approaches. Taking ergotropy as a central figure of merit, we characterize the injected and extractable energy, and identify regimes where charging remains robust despite environmental effects. Our results provide a systematic framework for assessing QB performance under thermal and dissipative effects.
summary: This paper explores how realistic environmental factors, such as temperature and decoherence, affect the charging of a quantum battery based on a kicked-Ising model. By using a mathematical framework involving Jordan-Wigner transformations, the authors show how energy injection and extractable work (ergotropy) decay under dissipation. They identify specific regimes where the battery's performance remains robust despite these effects. The work provides a systematic way to assess the viability of quantum batteries in noisy, real-world environments.
main_problem: Investigating the impact of thermal fluctuations and environmental dissipation (dephasing and relaxation) on the performance and robustness of periodically-kicked quantum batteries.
main_result: The study establishes that while temperature and dissipation reduce energy injection and ergotropy, the charging protocol remains robust under certain regimes, and provides an analytical framework to quantify these effects.
method: The authors use the Jordan-Wigner and Fourier transformations to map the system to free fermions, solving the Lindblad master equation via a system of coupled linear ordinary differential equations and applying the Cayley-Hamilton theorem.
model_or_system: A Kicked-Ising Chain (KIC) acting as a quantum battery, modeled by a Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) Hamiltonian subject to periodic kicks and open-system dynamics via the Lindblad master equation (including pure dephasing and thermal dissipation).
key_observables: Ergotropy (extractable work), injected energy, mean energy stored, fermion occupation number, and parity weights.
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), dephasing rate (gamma_z), dissipation rates (gamma_plus/minus), system size (N), and Ising interaction strength (J).
main_assumptions: The dynamics are modeled using the Lindblad master equation, and the dissipation is assumed to follow specific local-spin operators.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the evolution of normalized injected energy over time for different temperatures, the scaling of energy injection with system size, and a parameter map of energy injection across temperature and dephasing rate space.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the Kicked-Ising battery model, moves to the analytical derivation of the open-system dynamics using fermionization, presents the results for energy injection and ergotropy under thermal and dissipative effects, and concludes with a discussion on robustness and scaling.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides an exact analytical framework for studying how environmental noise and finite temperature degrade the performance of non-equilibrium quantum energy storage devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a dissipative kicked-Ising QB. Start- ing from a passive Gibbs state at temperature T = 1/β, the battery undergoes periodic driving in the presence of dissipa- tion. Decoherence mechanisms include pure dephasing, which suppresses quantum coherences at a rate γz, and excitation- relaxation processes, which induce transitions between energy levels at rates γ±, respectively.

FIG. 2. Normalized injected energies using (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the initial TFIM and evolving at the self-dual point. Dashed (solid) lines correspond to dynamics in the presence (absence) of dephasing. (a) For N = 100 spins, evolution for different β up to m = 100 kicks with γz = 0.005. (b) Normalized injected energies at m = N/2 for different system sizes across varying temperatures, with γz = 1/(10N). (c) Normalized injected energy in the presence of dephasing under different (β, γz) pairs. All data are obtained analytically [12].

FIG. 3. (a) Normalized injected energies (solid) and ergotropies (dash-dotted) in the presence of dephasing using (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the initial TFIM Hamiltonian and evolving at the self-dual point (J, b) = (π/4, −π/4) using N = 10 spins up to m = 10 kicks with γz = 0.01. (b) Same analysis as in (a), but for different system sizes N and temperatures β at kick m = N/2. (c) Normalized injected energy at the kick m = N/2 of a KIC QB with N = 10 in the presence of dephasing under various (β, γz) pairs. We set (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the TFIM Hamiltonian. (d) Same study as (c), but computing the ergotropy.

FIG. 4. (a) Normalized injected energies (solid) and ergotropies (dash-dotted) in the presence of thermal dissipation using (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the initial TFIM Hamiltonian and evolving at the self-dual point (J, b) = (π/4, −π/4). Results are shown for N = 10 spins, up to m = 10 kicks with γ = 0.01. (b) Same analysis as (a) but for different system sizes N across different temperatures β at the kick m = N/2. (c) Normalized injected energy at the kick m = N/2 of a KIC QB with N = 10 in the presence of thermal dissipation under various (β, γ) pairs. We set (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the TFIM Hamiltonian. (d) Same study as (c), but computing the ergotropy.
